"not_pressed", "scrollable-vertical": false}, {"text": "One area school district is working to bring more resources to families who've immigrated from other countries. In the last few years, Galesburg has seen a sharp increase of people from the Democratic Republic of the Congo. While students have English as a Second Language, or ESL courses, school district leaders noticed many parents struggling to communicate as well. The board on Monday night approved using about $6,900 in Title grant funds to try a 10-week pilot program for parents who need help learning English. \"What started out as a way to have the parents help the children, we're now looking at how can we acclimate them to the community, provide them some help learning the language so they can become employed and hopefully will stay here,\" said Superintendent Ralph Grimm. The pilot program should get underway mid-May. Classes will be two hours long and three times a week, with the option to go to a morning or a night session. Participants will pay a small fee to cover materials and an additional fee for childcare. Superintendent Grimm says the Regional Office of Education is also working on grant applications in hopes to expand this to a county-wide program.", "resource-id": "not_pressed", "scrollable-vertical": false}, {"text": "At Monday's Davenport School Board Meeting, the board passed an amendment to the current fiscal year 2016/2017 budget along with a $266 million dollar budget for fiscal year 2017-2018. In that budget, the board is spending $2.7 million in reserve funding, which is technically illegal in the eyes of the state of Iowa. Due to declining enrollment and lack of state funding due to an \"unfair\" funding formula, the district plans to make major cuts. Those involve $18 million dollars in cuts over the next three fiscal years. The district does not plan to fire any teachers, instead teaching positions will be cut through attrition as teachers retire. To help curb the funding loss for fiscal year 17/18 the district is increasing the tax levy. For property tax payers in Davenport, it will increase $.04 for every $1,000 in assessed value. The budget passed unanimously and needs to be sent to the state by April, 15th. Also on the agenda Monday night, the board passed an agreement with Studio 483 Architects for a design to renovate the locker rooms, concession stands and bathrooms underneath the bleachers at Brady Street Stadium. true, "bounds": [403, 2276, 1422, 2339], "pressed": "not_pressed", "scrollable-vertical": false}, {"text": "Illinois is going on two years without a budget and now lawmakers throughout state are on Spring Break from session. During that time, area legislators are meeting with business leaders in the Quad Cities. Sen. Neil Anderson and Representatives Tony McCombie, Daniel Swanson, and Mike Halpin were all at Monday's Legislative Luncheon at Jumer's Casino in Rock Island They believe there's a lot of work left to do in Springfield, but they're \"cautiously optimistic\" about some budget packages in the works. \"We've seen a significant set back with the failure of the senate package and it was really disappointing, it really did set us back a lot,\" Halpin said. With bipartisan agreement Halpin, McCombie, Swanson and Anderson say they are optimistic a budget could pass this session. \"The Senate's working on a budget package, we need to get an opportunity to at least vote up or down, bring it to the house and let us vote on an idea or vote on a budget,\" Swanson said. Anderson believes those budget packages being discussed could work with compromise from both sides of the aisle. \"The Senate Republicans have offered two budget solutions, one that's all cuts with no tax increases, and another one that kind of mimics the grand bargain that has tax increase and some reforms,\" Anderson said. McCombie believes those are the \"true options\" the state needs. \"I think that's what we have to do now, we have to forget about those that are higher than us and the food chain and just do it ourselves because it's not going to get done otherwise,\" McCombie said. \"I hope people understand that there are a minority of us on both sides of the aisle that are out there trying to do the right thing,\" Anderson added. The lack of a budget is also taking it's toll on many organizations in the Quad City area, like Family Resources. Nicole Cisne Durbin, the program officer at Family Resources says, for some things, they can use federal funding, but for the most part, they rely on state funding to pay their employees and to provide many of their services. She says without a budget, many of their employees are providing services in the Quad Cities without being paid. "bounds": [403, 743, 1422, 806], "componentLabel": "Text"}, {"text": "One area school district is working to bring more resources to families who've immigrated from other countries. In the last few years, Galesburg has seen a sharp increase of people from the Democratic Republic of the Congo. While students have English as a Second Language, or ESL courses, school district leaders noticed many parents struggling to communicate as well. The board on Monday night approved using about $6,900 in Title grant funds to try a 10-week pilot program for parents who need help learning English. \"What started out as a way to have the parents help the children, we're now looking at how can we acclimate them to the community, provide them some help learning the language so they can become employed and hopefully will stay here,\" said Superintendent Ralph Grimm. The pilot program should get underway mid-May. Classes will be two hours long and three times a week, with the option to go to a morning or a night session. Participants will pay a small fee to cover materials and an additional fee for childcare. Superintendent Grimm says the Regional Office of Education is also working on grant applications in hopes to expand this to a county-wide program.", "resource-id": "com.youngmedia.kwqc:id/video_item_details", "ancestors": ["android.widget.TextView", "android.view.View", "java.lang.Object"], "clickable": false, "class": "android.support.v7.widget.AppCompatTextView", "bounds": [403, 806, 1422, 1088], "componentLabel": "Text"}], "componentLabel": "List Item"}, {"ancestors": ["android.view.ViewGroup", "android.view.View", "java.lang.Object"], "class": "android.widget.LinearLayout", "bounds": [0, 1100, 1440, 1610], "clickable": false, "children": [{"ancestors": ["android.widget.ImageView", "android.view.View", "java.lang.Object"], "resource-id": "com.youngmedia.kwqc:id/video_thumbnail", "clickable": false, "class": "android.support.v7.widget.AppCompatImageView", "bounds": [18, 1122, 368, 1317], "componentLabel": "Image"}, {"text": "University of Iowa student death investigation", "resource-id": "com.youngmedia.kwqc:id/video_title_text", "ancestors": ["android.widget.TextView", "android.view.View", "java.lang.Object"], "clickable": false, "class": "android.support.v7.widget.AppCompatTextView", "bounds": [403, 1111, 1422, 1254], "componentLabel": "Text"}, {"text": "Apr 11, 2017 2:49 AM GMT+00:00", "resource-id": "com.youngmedia.kwqc:id/video_date_text", "ancestors": ["android.widget.TextView", "android.view.View", "java.lang.Object"], "clickable": false, "class": "android.support.v7.widget.AppCompatTextView", "bounds": [403, 1254, 1422, 1317], "componentLabel": "Text"}, {"text": "A male student who was found unresponsive in a residence hall at the University of Iowa and later died has been identified. A university spokesperson says 18-year-old Sean Wu was found in Daum Hall where he lived, after authorities responded to a call Saturday, April 8, 2017. University of Iowa police received a report of an unresponsive person around 10:44 p.m. The caller said the male had been drinking earlier in the evening. Witnesses said the student suddenly tensed up, as if he was having a seizure and collapsed. Wu was taken to the hospital where he later died. In that budget, the board is spending $2.7 million in reserve funding, which is technically illegal in the eyes of the state of Iowa. Due to declining enrollment and lack of state funding due to an \"unfair\" funding formula, the district plans to make major cuts. Those involve $18 million dollars in cuts over the next three fiscal years. The district does not plan to fire any teachers, instead teaching positions will be cut through attrition as teachers retire. To help curb the funding loss for fiscal year 17/18 the district is increasing the tax levy. For property tax payers in Davenport, it will increase $.04 for every $1,000 in assessed value. The budget passed unanimously and needs to be sent to the state by April, 15th. Also on the agenda Monday night, the board passed an agreement with Studio 483 Architects for a design to renovate the locker rooms, concession stands and bathrooms underneath the bleachers at Brady Street Stadium. During that time, area legislators are meeting with business leaders in the Quad Cities. Sen. Neil Anderson and Representatives Tony McCombie, Daniel Swanson, and Mike Halpin were all at Monday's Legislative Luncheon at Jumer's Casino in Rock Island They believe there's a lot of work left to do in Springfield, but they're \"cautiously optimistic\" about some budget packages in the works. \"We've seen a significant set back with the failure of the senate package and it was really disappointing, it really did set us back a lot,\" Halpin said. With bipartisan agreement Halpin, McCombie, Swanson and Anderson say they are optimistic a budget could pass this session. \"The Senate's working on a budget package, we need to get an opportunity to at least vote up or down, bring it to the house and let us vote on an idea or vote on a budget,\" Swanson said. Anderson believes those budget packages being discussed could work with compromise from both sides of the aisle. \"The Senate Republicans have offered two budget solutions, one that's all cuts with no tax increases, and another one that kind of mimics the grand bargain that has tax increase and some reforms,\" Anderson said. McCombie believes those are the \"true options\" the state needs. \"I think that's what we have to do now, we have to forget about those that are higher than us and the food chain and just do it ourselves because it's not going to get done otherwise,\" McCombie said. \"I hope people understand that there are a minority of us on both sides of the aisle that are out there trying to do the right thing,\" Anderson added. The lack of a budget is also taking it's toll on many organizations in the Quad City area, like Family Resources. Nicole Cisne Durbin, the program officer at Family Resources says, for some things, they can use federal funding, but for the most part, they rely on state funding to pay their employees and to provide many of their services. She says without a budget, many of their employees are providing services in the Quad Cities without being paid. Cisne Durbin says the non-profit is constantly assessing how much longer they can go on like this, but hopes the end is in sight. \"When one domino falls it's going to put more work on the other providers. because we're all working with similar populations, so it's really important to our community those who are most vulnerable in our community to have a budget that adequately funds all of our non-profit family serving services,\" Cisne Durbin said. Illinois Gov.
